items.question: Who uses maximum likelihood estimation in machine learning?
items.answer: Statisticians derive closed-form estimators; ML engineers minimize negative log-likelihood (cross-entropy, token NLL) via gradient descent when models are neural or high-dimensional.

items.question: What is the maximum likelihood estimator?
items.answer: θ̂_MLE = argmax_θ L(θ) where L(θ) = P(data | θ); equivalently θ̂_MLE = argmax_θ ℓ(θ) with log-likelihood ℓ(θ) = Σᵢ log P(xᵢ | θ).

items.question: When is closed-form MLE preferred over numerical optimization?
items.answer: When the model is simple enough that setting ∇ℓ(θ) = 0 yields an analytic solution—Bernoulli p, Gaussian μ, Exponential λ—avoiding iterative optimizers and guaranteeing the exact critical point.

items.question: Where does MLE appear in a deep learning training loop?
items.answer: After the forward pass, the loss is average −ℓ(θ) (e.g. cross-entropy); backward computes ∇(−ℓ) and the optimizer updates θ to increase ℓ(θ) on the mini-batch.

items.question: Why maximize log-likelihood instead of likelihood?
items.answer: Log is strictly increasing so argmax L = argmax ℓ, and ℓ turns products into sums—stable numerically, additive across i.i.d. samples, and easier to differentiate for gradient methods.

items.question: How do you compute Bernoulli MLE from n labeled examples?
items.answer: Count successes k among n trials; maximize ℓ(p) = k log p + (n−k) log(1−p) over p ∈ [0,1] to get p̂_MLE = k/n, the sample proportion of ones.

items.question: How does maximizing ℓ(θ) compare to minimizing negative log-likelihood?
items.answer: They are equivalent objectives: θ̂ = argmax_θ ℓ(θ) = argmin_θ [−ℓ(θ)]. Optimization libraries minimize, so NLL is the sign-flipped form plugged into gradient descent.

items.question: MLE vs ordinary least squares in linear regression—when do they coincide?
items.answer: Under i.i.d. Gaussian noise y = wᵀx + ε with ε ~ N(0, σ²) and fixed σ², minimizing MSE is MLE for w and b. Poisson or heavy-tailed noise breaks that equivalence and requires a different ℓ(θ).
